critique.summary: The article discusses the progress and current status of the CPython JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler for Python 3.15, highlighting that it has achieved modest but meaningful performance improvements ahead of schedule. It emphasizes the community-driven development, key technical changes like trace recording and reference count elimination, and the importance of the contributors and collaborative efforts behind the project.
mainPoints: Python 3.15's JIT is about 11-12% faster on macOS AArch64 and 5-6% faster on x86_64 Linux compared to the standard interpreter, with performance varying widely across benchmarks.
The JIT project faced challenges including loss of main funding but continued through community stewardship and volunteer contributions.
Key technical improvements include rewriting the JIT frontend to a tracing JIT and implementing reference count elimination to reduce overhead.
The project increased the number of active contributors by breaking down complex tasks into manageable issues and fostering a welcoming community.
Infrastructure improvements such as daily performance testing have been crucial for tracking progress and preventing regressions.
Collaboration and knowledge sharing with other compiler experts and projects like PyPy have helped improve the JIT development.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article align with publicly available information about the CPython JIT project and its development timeline. The performance improvements cited are consistent with data from independent benchmarking sites like doesjitgobrrr.com, which tracks CPython JIT performance. The article is authored by a known contributor to the CPython JIT project and provides detailed technical explanations and contributor acknowledgments, lending credibility. However, the article is a personal blog post and does not cite multiple independent sources or perspectives, which limits its journalistic reliability but does not undermine the factual accuracy of the technical content.
considerations: The performance improvements are preliminary and vary widely depending on the workload, so real-world gains may differ.
The article does not provide independent verification or external expert commentary on the JIT's performance or impact.
The discussion focuses on positive progress and may understate ongoing challenges such as lack of free-threading support or potential regressions.
The community-driven nature of the project is emphasized, but the sustainability and long-term funding situation remain unclear.
The article does not compare CPython's JIT approach in detail with other Python implementations like PyPy or alternative JIT technologies.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Python's JIT development progress, challenges in optimizing Python due to its dynamic features, and comparisons with other languages' JIT implementations. Several commenters express curiosity about technical details and the potential impact of the JIT on Python's future performance and ecosystem.

comments.author: rslashuser
comments.text:
I'm curious is the JIT developers could mention any Python features that prevent promising JIT features. An earlier Ken Jin blog [1], mentions how __del__ complicates reference counting optimization.

There is a story that Python is harder to optimize than, say, Typescript, with Python flexibility and the C API getting mentioned. Maybe, if the list of troublesome Python features was out there, programmers could know to avoid those features with the promise of activating the JIT when it can prove the feature is not in use. This could provide a way out of the current Python hard-to-JIT trap. It's just a gist of an idea, but certainly an interesting first step would be to hear from the JIT people which Python features they find troublesome.

[1] https://fidget-spinner.github.io/posts/faster-jit-plan.html
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully identifies specific Python features that complicate JIT optimization and suggests a practical approach to improving JIT adoption by avoiding problematic features.

comments.author: mattclarkdotnet
comments.text:
Python really needs to take the Typescript approach of "all valid Python4 is valid Python3". And then add value types so we can have int64 etc. And allow object refs to be frozen after instantiation to avoid the indirection tax.

Sensible type-annotated python code could be so much faster if it didn't have to assume everything could change at any time. Most things don't change, and if they do they change on startup (e.g. ORM bindings).
comments.analysis: The suggestion to adopt backward-compatible typing and value types is a reasonable proposal for improving Python's performance, reflecting a good understanding of language evolution and optimization.

comments.author: ecshafer
comments.text: What is wrong with the Python code base that makes this so much harder to implement than seemingly all other code bases? Ruby, PHP, JS. They all seemed to add JITs in significantly less time. A Python JIT has been asked for for like 2 decades at this point.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid question about Python's complexity and historical challenges in JIT implementation compared to other dynamic languages, highlighting known difficulties.

comments.author: owaislone
comments.text: Oh man, Python 2 > 3 was such a massive shift. Took almost half a decade if not more and yet it mainly changing superficial syntax stuff. They should have allowed ABIs to break and get these internal things done. Probably came up with a new, tighter API for integrating with other lower level languages so going forward Python internals can be changed more freely without breaking everything.
comments.analysis: Offers a reasonable historical perspective on Python's evolution and how stricter backward compatibility may have hindered internal improvements like JIT integration.

comments.author: qy-mj
comments.text: What performance improvements will I see if I upgrade from version 3.10 to 15?
comments.analysis: A straightforward question about practical benefits of upgrading, showing user interest but lacking technical depth.

comments.author: pjmlp
comments.text:
Great to see this going, Python also deserves a JIT, and given that only few bother with PyPy or GraalPy, shipping into the CPYthon is the only way to have less "rewrite into XYZ".

Kudos to those involved into making it happen.
comments.analysis: Expresses positive sentiment and support for integrating JIT into CPython, emphasizing practical ecosystem benefits.

comments.author: ekjhgkejhgk
comments.text: Doesn't PyPy already have a jit compiler? Why aren't we using that?
comments.analysis: Raises a common question about existing JIT solutions like PyPy, highlighting the distinction between alternative implementations and CPython's JIT efforts.

comments.author: ghm2199
comments.text:
Thanks for all the amazing work! I have Noob question. Wouldn't this get the funding back? Or would that not be preferable way to continue(as opposed to just volunteer driven)?

Like this is a big deal to get a project to a state where volunteers are spun up and actively breaking tasks and getting work done, no? It's a python JIT something I know next to nothing about — as do most application developers — which tells one how difficult this must have been.
comments.analysis: Shows curiosity about project funding and sustainability, reflecting awareness of open source development dynamics but speculative about funding outcomes.

comments.author: vanderZwan
comments.text:
> However, I misunderstood and came up with an even more extreme version: instead of tracing versions of normal instructions, I had only one instruction responsible for tracing, and all instructions in the second table point to that. Yes I know this part is confusing, I’ll hopefully try to explain better one day. This turned out to be a really really good choice. I found that the initial dual table approach was so much slower due to a doubling of the size of the interpreter, causing huge compiled code bloat, and naturally a slowdown.

> By using only a single instruction and two tables, we only increase the interpreter by a size of 1 instruction, and also keep the base interpreter ultra fast. I affectionally call this mechanism dual dispatch.

I really do hope they'll write that better explanation one day because this sounds pretty intriguing all on its own.
comments.analysis: Engages with technical details and requests clearer communication, indicating interest in deeper understanding of JIT internals.

comments.author: killingtime74
comments.text: Sorry but the graphs are completely unreadable. There are four code names for each of the lines. Which is jit and which is cpython?
comments.analysis: Provides useful feedback on presentation clarity, pointing out a common issue with technical articles that can hinder comprehension.
